intro: The clearest split is that 100ms uses a subscription model and Dyte uses a freemium model. 100ms is Real-time / WebSocket Infrastructure tool for developers; Dyte is Video call and audio room SDK with custom UI components, recording, and live streaming. The data points 100ms at chat applications and live collaboration features, and Dyte at chat applications and live collaboration features. Pricing: 100ms is subscription from $500 per month; Dyte is freemium from $0.00. Lock-in is low for 100ms and medium for Dyte, with DX at 4/5 vs 5/5 and transparency at 3/5 vs 5/5. On listed strengths, 100ms emphasises chat applications and live collaboration features, and Dyte emphasises chat applications and live collaboration features. Only 100ms lists an enterprise tier in the data. Honest trade-off: neither fits static projects, so if that is your context, look elsewhere; otherwise pick on the pivotal factor above and the DX score that matters most to your team.
quick_take: 100ms is for chat applications; Dyte is for chat applications; decide based on which pricing model fits your budget.
verdict_a: Choose 100ms if a subscription pricing model fits your procurement and your workload matches chat applications. Skip it if your context is static projects.
verdict_b: Choose Dyte if a freemium pricing model fits your procurement and your workload matches chat applications. Skip it if your context is static projects.

faq.a: Listed entry pricing is $500 per month for 100ms and $0.00 for Dyte. Both offer a free tier for getting started. Total cost at scale depends on usage and plan details that are not in this dataset, so confirm with each vendor.

faq.q: Can I migrate from 100ms to Dyte?
faq.a: 100ms is tagged with low lock-in (proprietary real-time sdk with custom session model; migration requires rewrite). Dyte is tagged with medium lock-in (sdk tied but data portable). Plan for SDK replacement, data export, and a parallel-run period; the dataset does not include migration tooling details.

faq.q: Which has better developer experience, 100ms or Dyte?
faq.a: The dataset rates Dyte at 5/5 for developer experience and 100ms at 4/5, so Dyte scores higher. That score reflects overall polish rather than any single integration, so trial both against your stack before committing.

faq.q: Is Dyte a good alternative to 100ms?
faq.a: Yes for teams whose workload matches chat applications, since both list the same fit. Dyte is flagged as not for static projects, so confirm your context does not fall there.
